speaker: Speaker 1
text: Welcome to the documentary podcast from the BBC World Service.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: My name is Justice Badoo and this is Africa's football dreamers.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: I'm standing just outside a local church in Domi Pilla 2. It's in the heart of Akra, Ghana's capital. Right in front of me is about 20 young kids who are running around after a football that is just about the size of a tennis ball.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: So I want to have a bit of a play with you guys, right?

speaker: Speaker 1
text: Okay. So let's go.

speaker: Speaker 2
text: Okay.

speaker: Speaker 3
text: Okay.

speaker: Speaker 4
text: The ball is my name.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: The ball is so tiny. It's actually even hard to find where it is.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: It's really memorable watching and taking part in this. It sends me back to more than 20 years ago when I was about 10 years old, playing in the village where I grew up.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: And this right here is the start of our journey that I'm going to take you on over the next 1 hour. My name is Justice Bado and in this documentary for the BBC World Service, I will be asking how you can take a boy or girl from a neighborhood like this one and transform him or her into a global football star.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: My name is Justice Bado and in this documentary for the BBC World Service, I will be asking how you can take a boy or girl from a neighborhood like this one and transform him or her into a global football star.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: How do you give them the confidence, the resilience, the mental fortitude and the skill to go through the journey of becoming a football icon?

speaker: Speaker 5
text: Football for them, it means everything.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: I've been given unprecedented access to one of Africa's top football schools, the Right to Dream Academy in Ghana.

speaker: Speaker 5
text: We truly believe that there's talent in Africa and we are changing football. We are challenging the narrative of that.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: I will be traveling with their scouts as they look for the next World Cup superstars in rural Ghana.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: Meeting their top talents as well as players taking their first steps in the professional game.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: This is Africa's football dreamers.

speaker: Speaker 5

speaker: Speaker 1
text: That intricate tiki-taka style, and when you combine it with our natural athleticism, it's really no wonder that so many of the world's top players have Ghanaian heritage.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: You might think of today's stars like Manchester City's Antoine Seme and Jeremy Doku.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: But also legends like Michael Essien, Abedi Pele, Sammy Kuffour, and Tony Yeboah, to name a few.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: Then there are all the players who have passed through this academy.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: A lineup that includes Ghana internationals Mohammed Kudus and Kamaldeen Sulemana, and Ivorian stars like Simon Adingra and Mohammed Diomande.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: They are four of more than 260 students who have gone on to forge careers as professional players.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: So I asked Alex, what is this academy all about?

speaker: Speaker 5
text: It's about helping young people achieve their dreams in a nutshell and achieving their dreams on the field and off the field.

speaker: Speaker 5
text: I think that with a lot of academies it's more on the field.

speaker: Speaker 5
text: What we've done that is special is that there is an education pillar apart from the football pillar, and we put as much emphasis on education as we put in football.

speaker: Speaker 5
text: So the bottom line is that we have a no child left behind policy.

speaker: Speaker 5
text: So that's what makes this place unique.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: After all, even at this elite level, only a small number of teenagers are going to make it as pros. And being released from an academy can be a crushing blow

speaker: Speaker 1
text: And being released from an academy can be a crushing blow to a child who has pinned all their hopes on making it in football.

speaker: Speaker 1

speaker: Speaker 1
text: This school was the first soccer academy in Africa to recruit girl players.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: And 13 of the 61 students are female.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: Right to Dream make their no child left behind policy work by selecting students who show exceptional footballing and academic promise.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: And to find them, they have a scouting program which takes them to towns and villages across Ghana, where they watch thousands of children compete in trials for a fully paid scholarship to the school.

text: I joined the school on a scouting trip,

speaker: Speaker 1

speaker: Speaker 1
text: Too often scouts have come to poor families with promises of football stardom that come to nothing.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: World football's governing body, FIFA has rules that ban players moving abroad to play for clubs until they are 18.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: But families in Ghana are often unaware of those rules.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: And while European clubs must only work with licensed agents, that doesn't stop rogue operators from selling false dreams.

speaker: Speaker 35
text: The unchanging coastline of the Gulf of Guinea borders a British colony, the Gold Coast.

speaker: Speaker 35
text: With the prospect of great changes ahead of it. Changes that would bring to its new future a new name, Ghana.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: Ghana, which was formerly known as Gold Coast, was a colony until 1957.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: For the final century belonging to Britain.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: This contact with Europeans is, of course, how we got into football in the first place.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: But it's a dark history.

speaker: Speaker 35
text: Dotted along the coast are the old trading forts built by Europeans.

speaker: Speaker 35
text: The most profitable form of trade was not in gold, but in men.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: For 400 years, Gold Coast endured the harsh realities of the global slave trade. Men and women selected for their physical attributes were sold and shipped abroad.

speaker: Speaker 35
text: They often spent months in the dungeons of these forts before being taken in chains from their homelands across the ocean to the West Indies and North America.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: This brutal history inevitably colors how I think about football migration.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: It is so important that Ghanaian footballers have a say in their future,

speaker: Speaker 1
text: that they are fully rewarded for their value.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: It was against this background of exploitation that the Right to Dream Football Academy was set up more than 25 years ago.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: And it's this background that explains why the school emphasizes education as well as football.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: Right to Dream is a high pressure environment with long days of training.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: As with any elite sports team, there is pressure and a focus on discipline.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: But some former students have said that at times, this went over the line, that the academy pushed them too hard

speaker: Speaker 1
text: and even used harsh corporate punishment

speaker: Speaker 1
text: when they stepped out of line.

speaker: Speaker 56
text: My name is Jalaus Brock,

speaker: Speaker 56
text: and I'm a journalist with the Danish newspaper Production.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: In 2018, Jeppe published an investigation into the system of discipline at Right to Dream Ghana.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: Using testimony from four former students,

speaker: Speaker 56
text: the main criticism from the players we talked to were the use of corporal punishment at the Right to Dream Academy in Ghana.

speaker: Speaker 56
text: If players did something that the leaders at the academy didn't like,

speaker: Speaker 56
text: it could be like using the phone during the daytime, it could be losing a ball or something like that.

speaker: Speaker 56
text: I just didn't show enough enthusiasm.

speaker: Speaker 56
text: They told us they could be punished, and the the most used punishment was what they called kneel down. So basically they were told to kneel in the baking sun for an undisclosed amount of time until the leader told them they could get back up and and move on with the day.

speaker: Speaker 56
text: And that could be one, two or three hours.

speaker: Speaker 56
text: They also told about incidents of uh where they had been canned,

speaker: Speaker 56
text: and one of them even slapped.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: After Jeppe published his story, Right to Dream issued a statement saying the allegations were false,

speaker: Speaker 1
text: exaggerated or taken out of context and had been made by disgruntled former students.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: However, they acknowledged a small number of incidents had happened years earlier, which went against their policies.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: Right to Dream declined to discuss the allegations with us for this program.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: These events were alleged to have taken place around a decade ago.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: And I saw no signs of this harsh punishment on my three visits to the Right to Dream Academy in 2025.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: Indeed, in the last 10 years, Right to Dream has changed a lot.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: The Ghana Academy has a new boss, Alex Edu, who we heard from earlier.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: And it's opened sister schools in Denmark, Egypt and the United States.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: This global network gives Right to Dream players the opportunity to join something called the International Academy,

speaker: Speaker 1
text: or IA as the players call it.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: This is effectively an under-18 squad which travels abroad for games.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: One of the IA players is Collins, who is currently playing for the Right to Dream team in Denmark.

text: For them when we are in the winter time, it's the weather that they have to get used to because if they're going to play here in Europe, they have to get used to the weather, but they the number one thing will be the food because we grew up not having a lot of that option at each meal.

text: But adapting to a new culture is part of the goal of the International Academy.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: Under FIFA rules, players under 18 from outside Europe can't sign to European clubs.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: These rules are important and exist to try to stop trafficking.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: But this can arguably put international players at a disadvantage too.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: It means they don't get exposure to elite level European football or European culture until they are ready to start their professional careers.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: The IA addresses this issue.

speaker: Speaker 58
text: Why the IA is so unique and important is that by the time they turned 18,

speaker: Speaker 58
text: you are actually understanding the level of football, the level of the game.

speaker: Speaker 58
text: You are understanding their lifestyle.

speaker: Speaker 58
text: So when they move here, then life is already easier for them.

text: You're all right.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: And one of the stepping stones to get there is where he is right now in Denmark.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: In 2015, Right to Dream took an extraordinary step of purchasing FC Noydland, a club in Faurum, a small town in the north of Copenhagen, which plays in the Danish Superliga.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: Ben Ganna explained the thinking behind the purchase.

speaker: Speaker 60
text: The way is here is that our first team is the final piece on the pathway.

speaker: Speaker 60
text: Because the style of play is the same.

speaker: Speaker 60
text: It's a very simple adjustment, so they get the opportunity to play first team football very quickly in a style of play that they know.

speaker: Speaker 60
text: If they went elsewhere from IA to somewhere else, it could be a completely different style of play.

speaker: Speaker 60
text: And now you're relearning the game, you're adapting to new things, whereas I think that final step here to go and play first team football

speaker: Speaker 60
text: and get, you know, there's young players here that have got 50 to 100 games very, very quickly by the age of 19, 20.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: Although many Right to Dream players want a bigger stage than the Danish Superliga, it's been an important stepping stone for Ghanaian players,

speaker: Speaker 1
text: a way to get lots of playing time in a city they already know.

speaker: Speaker 1

text: I think when if you're a fan of FC Norland, you would want those players to stay within the club for maybe two years or three years even,

speaker: Speaker 1
text: My journey has taken me across Ghana and across the world.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: From the streets of Akra and the pothold pitches of rural villages to stadiums in Europe.

speaker: Speaker 1
text: Of the thousands of kids in Ghana that try to make it as professional footballers, only a tiny number get the opportunity, and it is hard work.

speaker: Speaker 1
